name: French
register: Reverent contemporary French Bible register in the Louis Segond / Nouvelle Édition de Genève 1979 tradition — the most-trusted and most-read French Protestant Bible — warmed toward the accessible, natural phrasing of the Segond 21 and la Bible du Semeur so that a French Christian reader hears the intimacy of a child addressing their own father, not a formal deity.
quote_convention: French guillemets « … » with a non-breaking space inside each mark; dialogue and speech commonly opened with an em-dash (—).
poetry_convention: Poetry set as indented verse lines, one poetic colon/stich per line, with continuation (runover) lines further indented; strophes separated by a blank line — as in the Segond/NEG Psalms layout.

glossary.note: Warm filial divine name; 'céleste' is the standard Segond adjective for the Father in heaven (cf. Mt 6:26), never 'Dieu'.
Primary filial name; identical to the opening of the Lord's Prayer 'Notre Père', instantly heard as personal address. NEVER Dieu / l'Éternel.
Definite filial form as in Jn 5:19–23; the article marks the known, beloved Father, not a title of deity.
Filial form without the possessive; keeps 'Père' as head noun so the filial stem is preserved.

Anchored to Louis Segond (Rom 8:15 / Mk 14:36) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "Abba, Père". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)
REVERSION TOKEN — the generic deity word; used only where English keeps God-as-God, never as a substitute for 'notre Père'.
REVERSION TOKEN — Segond's distinctive covenant-name rendering of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH). Contrast term only; the generic title 'Lord' for Jesus = Seigneur, distinct.
Standard Segond/NEG rendering (Jn 14:26); hyphenated form is the fixed liturgical spelling.
Relational (by belonging), not by-identity; keeps 'notre Père' intact so the filial link is explicit (cf. Mt 10:20).
Fixed Segond Christological title (Jn 3:14); lowercase 'homme' per French usage.
Absolute use for Jesus in relation to the Father (Jn 3:35); capital F distinguishes from a human son.
Traditional Christological title (Jn 3:18). 'de Dieu' is retained here as a formal title, not a reversion of the Father-name; reviewer to confirm vs 'le Fils du Père'.
Exact Segond/NEG wording of Jn 3:16 ('son Fils unique'); 'unique' carries the monogenēs sense of the one and only.
FHB renders 'kingdom of God' filially; 'royaume' is the Segond term, refitted with 'notre Père' (Jn 3:3,5).
Standard French form of the name.
Segond form; the article is used where the title sense is in view (Jn 3:28), bare 'Christ' as a name elsewhere.
Standard French transliteration (Jn 1:41), paired naturally with 'le Christ' as its Hebrew equivalent.
Fixed Segond term for berith/diathēkē (Lc 22:20 'la nouvelle alliance').
Renders hesed's loyal covenant love; chosen over Segond's older 'bonté' because 'amour fidèle' (as in modern French versions) keeps both the warmth and the loyalty. Reviewer to confirm vs 'bonté'.
Segond uses 'ému de compassion' for splanchnizomai; captures the gut-level tender mercy of racham. Alt 'tendresse' if a softer note is wanted.
Standard Segond rendering of tsedaqah/dikaiosynē (Mt 6:33).
Exact Segond/NEG wording of Jn 3:16.
Segond rendering of Jn 3:3,7 ('naître de nouveau'); 'de nouveau' is the received French form, over the alt 'naître d'en haut' (from above).
